---
title: "Income & Inequality"
description: "Incomes, wages, and the distribution of income — Canada and OECD peers"
---

These charts track incomes and wages — most in inflation-adjusted ("real")
terms — along with the Gini coefficient and relative poverty.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.config import (
    CANADA_COLOR, PEER_COLOR, HIGHLIGHT_WIDTH, PEER_WIDTH,
    HIGHLIGHT_COUNTRY, PEER_COUNTRIES, get_data_date, get_retrieved_date,
)
from pipeline.charts import peer_comparison_line, ranked_bar, single_line, single_bar
```

## Where Canada stands

A scorecard of where Canada ranks among the 17 peers on each measure (latest available year). Dots are ordered by rank, so **further right is more favourable for Canada** (lower-is-better measures are flipped accordingly). Canada is red, key comparators in colour; hover a dot for its value and rank.

```{python}
#| echo: false
from pipeline.charts import page_snapshot
page_snapshot("income").show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## Median after-tax income in Canada

Median after-tax income, in 2024 constant (inflation-adjusted) dollars — the
midpoint, where half are above and half below. **Use the dropdown to switch between
all recipients, economic families, and unattached individuals.** These are Statistics
Canada's income units, *not* equivalized per person: an *economic family* pools the
income of related people in a household, so family income runs well above that of
unattached individuals, with the combined figure in between. Read each as a typical
income for that unit.

```{python}
#| echo: false
mi_path = "data/income/statcan_median_income_by_family.csv"
if os.path.exists(mi_path):
    mi = pd.read_csv(mi_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    latest_year = int(mi["date"].dt.year.max())
    FAM = [("all", "All — families & individuals"), ("families", "Economic families"),
           ("individuals", "Unattached individuals")]
    SRC = (f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 11-10-0190-01 — median after-tax income, "
           f"2024 constant dollars.<br>Latest year {latest_year}; "
           f"accessed {get_retrieved_date(mi_path)}.")
    def _anns(col):
        # 2020 pandemic-supports callout (on the selected line) + the source note
        a = [dict(text=SRC, xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.20, yanchor="top",
                  showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))]
        v = mi[mi["date"].dt.year == 2020][col]
        if len(v):
            a.append(dict(x="2020-01-01", y=float(v.iloc[0]),
                text="2020: temporary<br>pandemic supports", showarrow=True, arrowhead=2,
                ax=-25, ay=-45, font=dict(size=10, color="#777"), arrowcolor="#999"))
        return a
    fig = go.Figure(go.Scatter(x=mi["date"], y=mi["all"], mode="lines+markers",
        line=dict(color=CANADA_COLOR, width=3), marker=dict(size=4),
        hovertemplate="%{x|%Y}: $%{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>"))
    fig.update_layout(
        plot_bgcolor="white", height=460,
        yaxis=dict(title="Median after-tax income (2024 constant $)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
                   tickformat="$,.0f"),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
        margin=dict(t=64, b=96, r=30),
        updatemenus=[dict(buttons=[dict(label=lbl, method="update",
            args=[{"y": [mi[col]]}, {"annotations": _anns(col)}]) for col, lbl in FAM],
            x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.14, yanchor="top", direction="down", showactive=True)],
        annotations=_anns("all"))
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Median income data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
The rise around **2020** largely reflects temporary pandemic income supports (e.g. CERB)
and shifts in market income and transfers — read it as a one-off, not a durable gain in
living standards.
:::

## Median household income by city

Median household total income by metropolitan area (2021 Census). For the full **neighbourhood-level** view — every census tract, where you can zoom right to your own street — open the dedicated map (kept on its own page so this one stays fast to load):

**[→ Median household income by neighbourhood (census-tract map)](neighbourhoods.qmd)**

```{python}
#| echo: false
import json
from pipeline.charts import choropleth_map
cma_geo_p = "data/geo/cma_2021.geojson"
cma_p = "data/geo/statcan_cma_indicators.csv"
if os.path.exists(cma_geo_p) and os.path.exists(cma_p):
    cgeo = json.load(open(cma_geo_p))
    cdf = pd.read_csv(cma_p, dtype={"cmauid": str}).dropna(subset=["median_income"])
    fig = choropleth_map(cgeo, cdf, "cmauid", "median_income", name_col="name",
        colorbar_title="Median<br>household<br>income", value_prefix="$", value_fmt=",.0f",
        colorscale="Viridis",
        zmin=float(cdf["median_income"].quantile(0.05)), zmax=float(cdf["median_income"].quantile(0.95)),
        source_note="Source: 2021 Census (Statistics Canada), by metropolitan area.")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "scrollZoom": True, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Income map not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_census_geo`.")
```

## Average annual wage

Average annual wages converted to US dollars at purchasing power parity and held
in constant prices, so levels can be compared across countries and over time.

```{python}
#| echo: false
wage_path = "data/income/oecd_avg_wage.csv"
if os.path.exists(wage_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(wage_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "avg_wage",
        title="", yaxis_title="USD PPP (constant prices)", show_average=True,
        initial_visible=["USA"])
    fig.update_layout(yaxis_tickformat="$,.0f", annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Average Annual Wages | Data as of: {get_data_date(wage_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Average wage data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Latest average annual wage: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(wage_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(wage_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "avg_wage", "USD PPP (constant prices)",
        f"Source: OECD Average Annual Wages | Data as of: {get_data_date(wage_path)}",
        tickformat="$,.0f", hover_template="%{y}: $%{x:,.0f}<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Real household disposable income per capita

Real (inflation-adjusted) household disposable income per person, indexed to 2007 = 100. Disposable income is what households actually have to spend or save after taxes and transfers — a more direct gauge of household living standards than GDP per capita, which can rise without households feeling better off. Because it is indexed, the lines show *growth* since 2007 rather than dollar levels.

```{python}
#| echo: false
disp_path = "data/income/oecd_disposable_income.csv"
if os.path.exists(disp_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(disp_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "disposable_income_index",
        title="", yaxis_title="Index (2007 = 100, real)", show_average=True,
        initial_visible=["USA"])
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Household Dashboard | Data as of: {get_data_date(disp_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Disposable income data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Growth in disposable income since 2007: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(disp_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(disp_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "disposable_income_index", "Index (2007 = 100)",
        f"Source: OECD Household Dashboard | Data as of: {get_data_date(disp_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.0f} (2007=100)<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Income inequality (Gini coefficient)

The Gini coefficient of disposable (after-tax, after-transfer) income runs from 0
(everyone has the same income) to 1 (one person has all of it). Lower values
indicate a more even distribution. The OECD collects these figures on each
country's own survey cycle, so some report only every few years; the chart shows
the countries that report regularly enough to trace a line.

```{python}
#| echo: false
gini_path = "data/income/oecd_gini.csv"
if os.path.exists(gini_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(gini_path)
    # Drop sparsely-reporting countries (review §246) so the chart isn't cluttered
    # with 1–5-point lines: keep only those reporting in ≥8 years.
    df = df.groupby("country_code").filter(lambda g: g["gini"].notna().sum() >= 8)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "gini",
        title="", yaxis_title="Gini coefficient (0–1)", show_average=True)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Income Distribution Database | Data as of: {get_data_date(gini_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Gini data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Latest Gini coefficient: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(gini_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(gini_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "gini", "Gini coefficient (lower = more equal)",
        f"Source: OECD Income Distribution Database | Data as of: {get_data_date(gini_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.3f}<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Redistribution: before and after taxes and transfers

The Gini coefficients above measure inequality of **disposable** income — what households have to spend after taxes and government transfers. Measuring inequality of **market** income instead — earnings from work and capital, before the government steps in — and comparing the two shows how much the tax-and-transfer system reduces inequality. For each country below, the orange dot is market-income inequality and the blue dot is disposable-income inequality; the line between them is the redistributive effect. Canada begins from relatively low market inequality and narrows it by a moderate amount — more than the United States, but less than the continental-European welfare states.

```{python}
#| echo: false
gm_path = "data/income/oecd_gini_market.csv"
if os.path.exists(gini_path) and os.path.exists(gm_path):
    gd = pd.read_csv(gini_path).sort_values("year").groupby("country_code").tail(1)
    gm = pd.read_csv(gm_path).sort_values("year").groupby("country_code").tail(1)
    j = (gm[["country_code", "gini_market"]]
         .merge(gd[["country_code", "gini", "country_name"]], on="country_code"))
    j["reduction"] = j["gini_market"] - j["gini"]
    j = j.sort_values("gini", ascending=False)        # most equal (low Gini) ends up at top
    order = j["country_name"].tolist()
    is_ca = (j["country_name"] == "Canada")
    figr = go.Figure()
    # connecting lines (one trace, Canada's drawn red so its row stands out)
    for _, r in j.iterrows():
        figr.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=[r["gini_market"], r["gini"]], y=[r["country_name"]] * 2,
            mode="lines", line=dict(color=CANADA_COLOR if r["country_name"] == "Canada" else "#cfcfcf",
                                    width=3 if r["country_name"] == "Canada" else 2),
            showlegend=False, hoverinfo="skip"))
    figr.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=j["gini_market"], y=j["country_name"], mode="markers",
        name="Market income (before taxes & transfers)",
        marker=dict(color="#e07b39", size=[12 if c else 9 for c in is_ca],
                    line=dict(color=CANADA_COLOR, width=[2 if c else 0 for c in is_ca])),
        hovertemplate="Market: %{x:.3f}<extra>%{y}</extra>"))
    figr.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=j["gini"], y=j["country_name"], mode="markers",
        name="Disposable income (after taxes & transfers)",
        marker=dict(color="#1f6e8c", size=[12 if c else 9 for c in is_ca],
                    line=dict(color=CANADA_COLOR, width=[2 if c else 0 for c in is_ca])),
        hovertemplate="Disposable: %{x:.3f}<extra>%{y}</extra>"))
    yr = int(j["year"].max()) if "year" in j else None
    figr.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=600,
        xaxis=dict(title="Gini coefficient (lower = more equal)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
        yaxis=dict(title="", categoryorder="array", categoryarray=order),
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, x=0),
        margin=dict(l=10, r=20, t=60, b=70))
    figr.add_annotation(
        text="Source: OECD Income Distribution Database (latest available year per country)",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.11, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    figr.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Market-income Gini data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Income distribution over time

A single inequality number hides how income is actually shared. The chart below shows the **average after-tax income of each tenth (decile)** of Canadians, in constant 2024 dollars — slide through the years to watch real incomes rise, with the top decile pulling furthest ahead.

```{python}
#| echo: false
adf_path = "data/income/income_deciles_avg.csv"
if os.path.exists(adf_path):
    adf = pd.read_csv(adf_path)
    DEC_ORDER = ["Lowest decile", "Second decile", "Third decile", "Fourth decile",
                 "Fifth decile", "Sixth decile", "Seventh decile", "Eighth decile",
                 "Ninth decile", "Highest decile"]
    LABELS = ["Lowest", "2nd", "3rd", "4th", "5th", "6th", "7th", "8th", "9th", "Highest"]
    piv = adf.pivot(index="year", columns="decile", values="avg_income")[DEC_ORDER]
    years = piv.index.tolist()
    ymax = float(piv.values.max())
    steps = [dict(method="restyle", label=str(int(yr)), args=[{"y": [piv.loc[yr].tolist()]}])
             for yr in years]
    fig = go.Figure(go.Bar(x=LABELS, y=piv.loc[years[-1]].tolist(), marker_color="#4e79a7",
        hovertemplate="%{x} decile: $%{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>"))
    fig.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=470,
        yaxis=dict(title="Average after-tax income (2024 $)", tickprefix="$", tickformat=",",
                   range=[0, ymax * 1.05], gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
        xaxis=dict(title="Income decile (lowest to highest tenth)"),
        sliders=[dict(active=len(years) - 1, currentvalue=dict(prefix="Year: ", font=dict(size=14)),
                      pad=dict(t=50), steps=steps)],
        margin=dict(t=30, b=150),
        annotations=[dict(text="Source: Statistics Canada, Table 11-10-0193-01 (average after-tax income, constant 2024 $).",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.42, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Income-distribution data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

The next chart shows the **share of all income** held by the top 10% and the bottom 40% of Canadians. Switch between **market income** (before taxes and transfers) and **after-tax income**: the market gap is wide and has widened since the 1970s, while the after-tax gap is far narrower — a direct picture of how the tax-and-transfer system redistributes income.

```{python}
#| echo: false
sh_path = "data/income/income_top_bottom_share.csv"
if os.path.exists(sh_path):
    sdf = pd.read_csv(sh_path)
    d0 = sdf[sdf["concept"] == "After-tax income"].sort_values("year")
    fig2 = go.Figure()
    fig2.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=d0["year"], y=d0["top10"], name="Top 10%", mode="lines",
        line=dict(color="#d62728", width=2.5), hovertemplate="Top 10%: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>"))
    fig2.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=d0["year"], y=d0["bottom40"], name="Bottom 40%", mode="lines",
        line=dict(color="#4e79a7", width=2.5), hovertemplate="Bottom 40%: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>"))
    buttons = []
    for concept, label in [("After-tax income", "After taxes & transfers"),
                           ("Market income", "Market (before tax & transfers)")]:
        t = sdf[sdf["concept"] == concept].sort_values("year")
        buttons.append(dict(method="update", label=label,
            args=[{"y": [t["top10"].tolist(), t["bottom40"].tolist()]}]))
    fig2.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=440, hovermode="x unified",
        yaxis=dict(title="Share of total income (%)", ticksuffix="%", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", rangemode="tozero"),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, x=0),
        updatemenus=[dict(buttons=buttons, active=0, x=1, xanchor="right", y=1.13, yanchor="top",
            bgcolor="white", bordercolor="#ccc", borderwidth=1, showactive=True)],
        margin=dict(t=60, b=90),
        annotations=[dict(text=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 11-10-0193-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(sh_path)}",
            xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.2, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig2.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Income-share data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Relative poverty rate

The relative poverty rate is the share of people living on less than half of the
national median disposable income. It measures low income relative to the
national standard of living rather than an absolute threshold.

```{python}
#| echo: false
pov_path = "data/income/oecd_poverty.csv"
if os.path.exists(pov_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(pov_path)
    # Drop sparsely-reporting countries (review §249), as on the Gini chart above.
    df = df.groupby("country_code").filter(lambda g: g["poverty_rate"].notna().sum() >= 8)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "poverty_rate",
        title="", yaxis_title="% below 50% of median income", show_average=True)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Income Distribution Database | Data as of: {get_data_date(pov_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Poverty data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Low-income rate in Canada (LIM-AT)

Statistics Canada's Low Income Measure, after tax (LIM-AT): the share of Canadians
in households below half the median adjusted household income. This is the
domestic counterpart to the OECD relative-poverty series above.

```{python}
#| echo: false
li_path = "data/income/statcan_low_income.csv"
if os.path.exists(li_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(li_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    fig = single_line(df, "date", "low_income_rate", title="",
                      yaxis_title="% of persons in low income (LIM-AT)",
                      rangeslider=True,
                      source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 11-10-0135-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date(li_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Low-income data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Food insecurity

The share of people in households that experienced food insecurity in the past year — worrying about running out of food, compromising on quality or quantity, or going without. It is a direct measure of material hardship that income and poverty lines alone can miss, and has risen sharply in recent years.

```{python}
#| echo: false
fi_path = "data/income/statcan_food_insecurity.csv"
if os.path.exists(fi_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(fi_path, parse_dates=["date"])
    fig = single_bar(df, "date", "food_insecurity_rate", title="",
                     yaxis_title="% of persons in food-insecure households",
                     color=CANADA_COLOR, yticksuffix="%", hover_fmt=".1f", rangeslider=True,
                     source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 13-10-0835-01 (Canadian Income Survey) | Data as of: {get_data_date(fi_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Food insecurity data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Download data

- [Median After-Tax Income (CSV)](../data/income/statcan_median_income.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 11-10-0190-01
- [Neighbourhood income, GeoJSON](../data/geo/ct_income_2021.geojson) — 2021 Census, median household income by census tract
- [Food Insecurity (CSV)](../data/income/statcan_food_insecurity.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 13-10-0835-01
- [Average Annual Wage (CSV)](../data/income/oecd_avg_wage.csv) — OECD Average Annual Wages
- [Real Household Disposable Income per Capita (CSV)](../data/income/oecd_disposable_income.csv) — OECD Household Dashboard
- [Gini Coefficient — disposable income (CSV)](../data/income/oecd_gini.csv) — OECD Income Distribution Database
- [Gini Coefficient — market income (CSV)](../data/income/oecd_gini_market.csv) — OECD Income Distribution Database
- [Average income by decile (CSV)](../data/income/income_deciles_avg.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 11-10-0193-01
- [Top-10% vs bottom-40% income share (CSV)](../data/income/income_top_bottom_share.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 11-10-0193-01
- [Relative Poverty Rate (CSV)](../data/income/oecd_poverty.csv) — OECD Income Distribution Database
- [Low-Income Rate, LIM-AT (CSV)](../data/income/statcan_low_income.csv) — Statistics Canada Table 11-10-0135-01
